findings.summary: infrastructure/llm imports PDFValidationError from infrastructure/validation instead of from infrastructure/core/exceptions where it is defined
evidence: generator.py line 55: `from infrastructure.validation.pdf_validator import extract_text_from_pdf, PDFValidationError`
pdf_validator.py line 21: `from infrastructure.core.exceptions import PDFValidationError` — the class lives in core/exceptions.py
generator.py line 485 catches PDFValidationError obtained via the re-export path through validation layer, creating a dependency from llm -> validation for a type that belongs to core
findings.suggestion: In generator.py, import PDFValidationError directly from infrastructure.core.exceptions (where it is defined) rather than re-importing it via infrastructure.validation.pdf_validator. This removes the llm->validation coupling for a pure type import.

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/__init__.py re-exports the full public surface of all 5 sub-packages (core, templates, validation, utils, review), creating a single hub that couples all sub-packages at import time
evidence: lines 30-121: __init__.py imports 80+ symbols from core, templates, validation, utils, and review sub-packages
Any import-time failure in any sub-package (e.g., ollama not installed) will cause the entire infrastructure.llm namespace to fail to import
The optional-import guard for PromptComposer/PromptFragmentLoader (lines 89-94) is the only mitigation, but no similar guard exists for the heavy ollama utils (lines 58-69)
findings.suggestion: Keep the __init__.py as a thin facade that only re-exports the most common entry points (LLMClient, LLMConfig, get_template). Move advanced symbols (Ollama utils, all validation constants, all metrics classes) to require direct sub-module imports. This reduces blast radius and avoids import-time failures propagating from one optional sub-package to all callers.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/doc_scanner.py uses deferred local imports inside methods rather than declaring its dependencies at module top level, obscuring the actual dependency graph
evidence: line 64: `from infrastructure.validation.doc_discovery import find_markdown_files` inside phase2_accuracy()
line 91: same import repeated inside phase4_quality()
line 212: `from infrastructure.validation.doc_discovery import find_markdown_files, identify_cross_references` inside _verify_cross_references()
findings.suggestion: Move all imports to module top level. The deferred import pattern is only justified when there is a genuine circular import; no such cycle exists here (doc_discovery does not import doc_scanner). Top-level imports make the dependency graph visible and allow linters/tools to detect missing deps at startup.

findings.summary: TypedDict classes are defined in some modules as plain data containers alongside functional code, while dataclasses are used in others for the same purpose, without a clear boundary rule
evidence: integrity.py lines 368-395: three TypedDict classes (BuildArtifactValidation, PermissionCheck, OutputCompleteness) defined in the middle of a functional module, after all the functions that use them
suite_summary_generator.py lines 18-30: InfraResults TypedDict defined near top before functions
error_aggregator.py uses @dataclass for ErrorEntry; pipeline_reporter.py uses @dataclass for PipelineReport — both in reporting/ — while suite_summary_generator uses TypedDict in the same package
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single structural container convention per package: prefer @dataclass for mutable aggregates (most reporting types) and TypedDict only for typed dict-return shapes. Place structural type definitions at the top of their module before functions. Consolidate data model classes into a dedicated models.py or similar per-package if multiple modules need the same shapes.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/ has two separate CLI modules (cli.py and validate_pdf_cli.py and validate_markdown_cli.py) following different patterns — one generic dispatcher, two standalone scripts — inconsistent with the single cli.py pattern used in other sub-packages
evidence: Other sub-packages (rendering, publishing, llm) expose a single cli.py or cli/ sub-package
validation/ has cli.py (generic dispatcher) plus validate_pdf_cli.py and validate_markdown_cli.py as standalone top-level scripts with overlapping responsibilities
findings.suggestion: Consolidate validate_pdf_cli.py and validate_markdown_cli.py into cli.py subcommands (already partially done for validate_pdf_cli.py which imports from cli.py). Remove the standalone entrypoints or reduce them to thin shims that delegate to cli.py.

findings.summary: review/generator.py uses three inconsistent failure strategies for different error paths: raise, return None, and return error-string — callers cannot predict which to expect
evidence: check_ollama_availability() lines 317-367: returns (False, None) on all error paths — no exception raised
extract_manuscript_text() line 459: returns (None, metrics) when file not found — no exception
generate_review_with_metrics() line 625: returns an error-string starting with '*Error generating...*' when all retries exhausted — caller must inspect string content to detect failure
save_single_review() line 476: raises Exception (re-raise) on file write failure — the one path that does raise
warmup_model() line 448: returns (False, 0.0) on failure — no exception
findings.suggestion: Establish one strategy per error category: use return tuple (success, value) for optional/degradable operations (warmup, ollama check), raise LLMError subclass for hard failures (file not found for required input, exhausted retries producing no usable output). Document the strategy in module docstring so callers know what to expect. Replace the error-string return from generate_review_with_metrics on retry exhaustion with raising LLMError.

findings.summary: Multiple modules in infrastructure/validation/ and infrastructure/reporting/ catch bare Exception and only log, with no propagation, silently masking failures mid-pipeline
evidence: doc_accuracy.py lines 187-188: `except Exception as e: logger.warning(f'Error checking links in {md_file}: {e}')` — returns empty result silently
doc_accuracy.py lines 238-239, 276-277: same pattern for command and path reference checks
doc_discovery.py lines 108-109: `except Exception as e: logger.debug(...)` — cross-reference scan failure is entirely silent at debug level
dashboard_generator.py has 11 bare `except Exception as e` blocks each logging.error() without re-raising or returning a failure signal
findings.suggestion: For I/O operations over individual files in a loop (doc_accuracy, doc_discovery), keep the swallow-and-continue pattern but elevate to WARNING and include the file path. For dashboard_generator functions that are called as pipeline stages, propagate a failure signal (return False or raise) instead of silently continuing — callers currently have no way to know a dashboard section failed.

findings.summary: Several raise-from-e patterns are missing in infrastructure/reporting/ and infrastructure/llm/ where custom exceptions are raised after catching lower-level ones, losing the original traceback
evidence: suite_summary_generator.py lines 372-373: `logger.error(...); raise` — correctly re-raises, but the OSError is re-raised without wrapping in a TemplateError, making the exception type inconsistent with the rest of the codebase

io.py line 199: `except Exception as e: logger.error(..., exc_info=True); success = False` — file save failure sets a flag but does not raise; io.py line 475: `except Exception as e: logger.error(...); raise` — same file, different function, raises. Two inconsistent patterns for the same type of I/O failure in the same module.
findings.suggestion: In io.py, standardize: save_single_review (line 475) raises on failure (correct), but save_review_outputs (line 199) only sets success=False. Either both should raise (to force callers to handle) or both should return a failure signal. Pick one and apply it consistently within the module.

findings.summary: Four review-type functions are pure forwarders with no added policy, each differing only in two literal arguments
evidence: Lines 656-715: generate_llm_executive_summary, generate_quality_review, generate_methodology_review, generate_improvement_suggestions each call generate_review_with_metrics with identical signatures, differing only in review_type string and template_class argument — no guard logic, no transformation, no validation is added by the wrapper layer.
The REVIEW_GENERATORS dict in pipeline_runner.py (lines 38-43) already parametrises these functions; the four separate wrapper functions exist only to give names to argument combinations.
findings.suggestion: Eliminate the four wrapper functions; populate REVIEW_GENERATORS directly with (review_type, template_class, temperature) tuples and call generate_review_with_metrics once from the loop.

findings.summary: LLMConfig.from_env() is called 5 times inside generator.py functions instead of being passed in or resolved once
evidence: Lines 296, 470, 571, 599, 728: each function resolves a fresh LLMConfig from the environment independently.
Line 599 is inside a retry loop — create_review_client at line 296 already builds a client from config; the inner loop re-instantiates config for no reason.
findings.suggestion: Accept a LLMConfig parameter at the entry points (generate_review_with_metrics, generate_translation) and thread it through; or resolve once at pipeline startup and pass to all generators.

findings.summary: Several DocumentationScanner phase-helper methods are stub placeholders returning hardcoded dicts, inflating the apparent abstraction surface
evidence: Line 205: _validate_markdown_syntax returns {'status': 'basic_validation_passed'} unconditionally.
Lines 207-209: _test_documented_commands returns {'status': 'manual_testing_required', 'commands_found': 0} unconditionally.
Lines 224-226: _check_circular_references returns {'status': 'no_circular_references_detected'} unconditionally.
All three methods pretend to check something but do nothing — callers in phase6_verification trust the returned dict.
findings.suggestion: Either implement the checks or remove the methods and omit their keys from the verification_results dict; stub methods that always return success silently suppress real failures.

findings.summary: doc_scanner.py has three deferred inline imports inside instance methods rather than at module level
evidence: Line 64 (phase2_accuracy): from infrastructure.validation.doc_discovery import find_markdown_files
Line 92 (phase4_quality): from infrastructure.validation.doc_discovery import find_markdown_files
Lines 213-216 (_verify_cross_references): from infrastructure.validation.doc_discovery import find_markdown_files, identify_cross_references
All three import the same symbol from the same module; it is also imported at module-level indirectly via run_discovery_phase. The deferred imports suggest incomplete refactoring.
findings.suggestion: Move find_markdown_files and identify_cross_references to the module-level imports block and remove the three inline import statements.

findings.summary: generator.py has 15+ distinct infrastructure sub-package imports, making it a coupling hub for the LLM subsystem
evidence: Lines 12-55: imports span infrastructure.core.logging_utils, infrastructure.core.logging_progress, infrastructure.llm.core.client, infrastructure.llm.core.config, infrastructure.llm.utils.ollama (6 symbols), infrastructure.llm.utils.heartbeat, infrastructure.llm.templates.manuscript (6 symbols), infrastructure.llm.validation.repetition, infrastructure.llm.validation.format, infrastructure.llm.review.metrics, infrastructure.validation.pdf_validator — 11 distinct import paths.
The optional import block (lines 60-66) adds two more conditional paths from infrastructure.llm.prompts.
findings.suggestion: Split check_ollama_availability and warmup_model into a separate ollama_setup.py module; this would reduce generator.py's fan-out and move Ollama-lifecycle imports away from the review-generation logic.

findings.summary: The prompt system availability is resolved at module import time via a try/except, creating a fragile import-time side effect
evidence: Lines 59-66: PROMPT_SYSTEM_AVAILABLE is set by importing infrastructure.llm.prompts at module load. If the module is partially initialised, the flag silently falls back to False.
Lines 70-75 and 495-500: two separate call sites check PROMPT_SYSTEM_AVAILABLE with identical fallback handling, duplicating the guard pattern.
findings.suggestion: Resolve prompt-system availability once at pipeline startup (in pipeline_runner.py) and pass a resolved PromptComposer or None into the functions that need it, removing the module-level flag.

findings.summary: doc_scanner coverage tests use hasattr guards that make every assertion conditional and allow tests to pass trivially when the API does not exist
evidence: test_doc_scanner_coverage.py lines 34-36, 40-42, 53-55, 57-59: every test body is wrapped in if hasattr(doc_scanner, '...') — if the attribute is absent the test passes with zero assertions.
test_doc_scanner_phases.py lines 20-22, 40-42, 45-47, 50-52: same pattern for _group_gaps_by_category, _group_gaps_by_severity, _create_hierarchy, phase5_improvements — all guarded with hasattr.
grep count shows 108 occurrences of 'if hasattr' across 19 test files, indicating this is a systemic pattern, not an isolated case.
findings.suggestion: Remove hasattr guards; import and call the actual public API unconditionally. If the API is intentionally optional, use pytest.importorskip or a fixture that skips cleanly, not a guard that masks missing coverage.

findings.summary: The central generate_review_with_metrics path has no unit-level test that exercises its retry logic, validation branching, or deduplication — only a slow Ollama integration test exists
evidence: test_review_generators.py line 65: @pytest.mark.slow test_generates_executive_summary requires a live ollama_test_server fixture and calls the full function end-to-end.

The retry loop (generator.py lines 590-628), off-topic path (lines 607-607), deduplication branch (lines 631-641), and _build_retry_prompt (lines 490-501) have no tests that can run without a network dependency.
validate_review_quality is tested in test_llm_review.py only via simple string fixtures — the retry integration between validation results and loop exit is not covered.
findings.suggestion: Extract a pure RetryController or make generate_review_with_metrics accept an injectable _stream_fn callable so the retry/dedup/validation logic can be driven by deterministic fixtures without Ollama.

findings.summary: estimate_tokens is tested identically in two separate test files with the same test cases
evidence: test_metrics.py TestEstimateTokens: tests empty string, 4 chars, 8 chars, 400 chars, return type, 3 chars.
test_llm_review.py TestEstimateTokens (lines 38-65): tests empty string, 12 chars, 400 chars, realistic text.
The core assertions (empty→0, 400 chars→100 tokens) appear in both; the second file adds nothing beyond what the first already verifies.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate estimate_tokens tests into test_metrics.py and delete the duplicate class from test_llm_review.py.

findings.summary: test_config_immutability asserts nothing about immutability and contains a misleading comment that admits the test is incomplete
evidence: Lines 152-159: the test is titled test_config_immutability, the comment says '(config is stored by reference, but we test the pattern)' and the only assertion is client.config.temperature == 0.5 — the same assertion made by test_config_property three lines earlier.
No mutation of config is attempted and no isolation is verified — the test title claims a contract that is not checked.
findings.suggestion: Either test the actual mutation scenario (modify config after client construction, assert client.config is unaffected if copy semantics are intended) or remove the test.

findings.summary: Section-banner comments in logging_utils.py restate what the function groups already make obvious
evidence: Lines 42-44: '# ============= LOG LEVEL CONFIGURATION =============' above a 5-line dict whose purpose is self-evident
Lines 55-57: '# ============= STANDARDIZED PROJECT LOGGING INTERFACE =============' above get_log_level_from_env, which documents itself
Lines 67-69: '# ============= LOGGER CONFIGURATION =============' repeated band before setup_logger
Lines 289-291: '# ============= UTILITY FUNCTIONS =============' banner above simple log_success
Lines 412-414: trailing comment 'Core logging API ...' after __all__ that re-explains what __all__ already communicates
findings.suggestion: Remove the ASCII-banner section dividers; the function names and docstrings already provide navigation cues. Reserve banners for genuinely large files that need explicit wayfinding.

findings.summary: Trivial one-liner functions carry multi-line Args/Returns docstrings that add no information
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py lines 134-143: _generate_pipeline_markdown has a full Args/Returns docstring on a private helper whose signature is self-documenting (PipelineReport -> str)
pipeline_reporter.py lines 84-96: save_pipeline_report docstring repeats parameter types already expressed in the signature annotations
logging_utils.py line 232: log_operation docstring is one line, but adjacent trivial helpers (log_substep, log_success) have equally verbose descriptions for 2-line bodies
findings.suggestion: For private helpers and trivial one-concept functions, use a single-line docstring or none. Reserve full Args/Returns blocks for public API functions with non-obvious contracts.

findings.summary: check_ollama_availability logs step-by-step progress ('Checking...', 'Discovering...', 'Selecting...') that reads as scaffolding commentary rather than operational signal
evidence: Lines 322-367: eight separate logger.info calls narrating internal steps ('Checking Ollama server status...', 'Discovering available models...', 'Selecting best model for manuscript review...')
This is a single function performing a sequential workflow; the per-substep narration only adds value during development, not in production
findings.suggestion: Collapse internal progress logs to DEBUG level; emit a single INFO on entry and one on success/failure. Use log_substep only at call sites where the caller is orchestrating multiple named operations.

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/__init__.py re-exports 60+ symbols from six subpackages, making the boundary between public API and internal details invisible
evidence: Lines 30-121: flat re-export of LLMClient, LLMConfig, GenerationOptions, ConversationContext, Message, ResponseMode, save_response, save_streaming_response, strip_thinking_tags, get_max_input_length, get_review_max_tokens, get_review_timeout, REVIEW_MIN_WORDS, TRANSLATION_LANGUAGES, 7 template classes, 14 validation functions, 10 Ollama utility functions, PromptFragmentLoader, PromptComposer, 4 metric dataclasses, and 13 review generation functions
The docstring at lines 20-24 itself says 'Direct imports (recommended)' — undercutting the purpose of the re-export surface
__all__ (lines 123-207) enumerates 84 names, longer than most source modules in the subpackage
findings.suggestion: Limit __init__.py to the 6-8 symbols that external callers actually need as a stable API boundary (LLMClient, LLMConfig, GenerationOptions, get_template, ensure_ollama_ready, OutputValidator). Route all other imports to their direct submodule paths.

findings.summary: The logging module is fragmented across logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, and logging_progress.py without a clear split criterion
evidence: logging_utils.py imports from all four sibling modules (lines 24-28) and re-exports format_duration (which lives in logging_helpers) via __all__ line 431 — so helpers is effectively an extension of utils
logging_progress.py contains calculate_eta, log_with_spinner, StreamingProgress, Spinner — mixed concern (ETA math vs. UI output)
The core/__init__.py documents these split submodules at lines 1-15 but still pulls from logging_utils as the entry point, meaning the split added files without removing the hub
findings.suggestion: Either consolidate logging_helpers and logging_constants back into logging_utils (they have negligible standalone size), or formalize the split with a dedicated logging/ subpackage and its own __init__.py. The current hybrid is the worst of both worlds.

findings.summary: suite_summary_generator.py and the removed test_summary_generator.py (referenced in git status) represent duplicated summary generation responsibility in the reporting package
evidence: suite_summary_generator.py (the file at path) implements generate_summary_report, generate_markdown_report, run_test_summary_generation — a complete self-contained test summary pipeline
Git status shows infrastructure/reporting/test_summary_generator.py as M (modified) — a sibling file with same stated purpose
The reporting package has 14 modules; having two named *_summary_generator.py for the same domain (test results) signals unclear ownership
findings.suggestion: Consolidate test result summary generation into a single module. Use a single public function generate_test_summary_report(repo_root) that is the sole entry point for all callers.

findings.summary: The reporting package mixes broad cross-cutting reporters (executive_reporter, multi_project_reporter) with narrow helpers (html_templates, output_organizer), making change boundaries unpredictable
evidence: 14 modules: __init__, test_runner, coverage_parser, pipeline_reporter, dashboard_generator, error_aggregator, html_templates, output_reporter, suite_summary_generator, output_organizer, manuscript_overview, coverage_reporter, executive_reporter, multi_project_reporter
html_templates.py is a pure data file (HTML strings); output_organizer.py is a filesystem utility — both belong in different packages by responsibility
dashboard_generator and executive_reporter serve overlapping roles (both produce summary views); no clear ownership boundary
findings.suggestion: Split reporting into reporting/pipeline/ (pipeline_reporter, error_aggregator), reporting/test/ (coverage_parser, coverage_reporter, suite_summary_generator), and reporting/output/ (output_reporter, output_organizer). Move html_templates into rendering/ where HTML generation is the primary responsibility.

findings.summary: verify_output_integrity calls four sub-verifiers then builds recommendations inline, conflating orchestration and policy in a single 60-line function
evidence: Lines 239-299: single function runs verify_file_integrity, verify_cross_references, verify_data_consistency, verify_academic_standards, accumulates issues into report, then generates three hardcoded recommendations

Lines 291-297: recommendation strings ('Fix integrity issues before proceeding', 'Check file permissions and paths', 'Verify all cross-references...') are always appended if any failure — these are static policy, not data-driven
The function both orchestrates and decides when to warn, mixing levels of abstraction
findings.suggestion: Extract the recommendations logic into a _generate_recommendations(report: IntegrityReport) -> list[str] helper. The orchestrator should only call sub-verifiers and populate the report fields; policy about what to recommend belongs in the helper.

findings.summary: generate_summary_report in suite_summary_generator.py constructs a deeply nested dict literal inline, mixing data assembly with orchestration logic
evidence: Lines 189-246: single function returns a dict with 8 top-level keys, each containing nested dicts, assembled from ~15 local variables
Lines 197-227: 'infrastructure' section and 'metadata' section are built inline with repeated .get() calls that mirror the InfraResults TypedDict fields already defined at lines 18-31
The metadata block (lines 212-220) hardcodes test_command string and boolean flags that are not computed from inputs — they describe the caller's intent, not this function's result
findings.suggestion: Define a dataclass or TypedDict for the report shape and populate it in a dedicated builder. Separate the data assembly from the orchestration (discover_projects -> load_results -> assemble_report -> return).

findings.summary: create_integrity_manifest casts manifest keys to int on each iteration unnecessarily, suggesting confusion about type guarantees
evidence: Lines 574-575: 'manifest["file_count"] = int(manifest["file_count"]) + 1' and 'manifest["total_size"] = int(manifest["total_size"]) + file_size'
The manifest dict is initialized at line 550-556 with integer literals (0, 0) — no cast is needed; the defensive int() wrapping implies uncertainty about initialization guarantees that doesn't exist
findings.suggestion: Remove the int() casts; initialize manifest with typed counters (file_count: int = 0, total_size: int = 0) and increment directly.

findings.summary: verify_data_consistency imports numpy inside the loop body on each .npz/.npy file, incurring repeated import resolution overhead
evidence: Lines 174-178: 'import numpy as np' is inside the for loop body, conditionally executed per file
Python caches module imports but the lookup still occurs inside the loop; more importantly, the pattern signals the author was uncertain where to put the import
findings.suggestion: Move the numpy import to the top of the file (already handled via _optional_deps pattern elsewhere in the codebase) or at least hoist it outside the loop.

findings.summary: check_file_permissions initializes 'executable': True in the result dict but never sets it to False in any code path, making the field meaningless
evidence: Lines 446-451: PermissionCheck dict initialized with 'executable': True
Lines 458-473: only 'readable' and 'writable' are conditionally set to False; 'executable' is never touched after initialization
The PermissionCheck TypedDict at lines 377-383 declares 'executable: bool' implying it should be validated
findings.suggestion: Either implement the executable check (os.access(output_dir, os.X_OK)) or remove the 'executable' field from both the TypedDict and the return dict if it is not used.

findings.summary: integrity.py mixes three different return conventions: TypedDict, plain dict, and dataclass — without a consistent pattern across functions at the same abstraction level
evidence: verify_file_integrity (line 43) returns dict[str, bool]
verify_cross_references (line 77) returns dict[str, bool]
verify_output_integrity (line 239) returns IntegrityReport dataclass
validate_build_artifacts (line 398) returns BuildArtifactValidation TypedDict
check_file_permissions (line 444) returns PermissionCheck TypedDict
verify_output_completeness (line 477) returns OutputCompleteness TypedDict
verify_integrity_against_manifest (line 610) returns dict[str, Any]
findings.suggestion: Choose one pattern for functions at the same level. Given that TypedDict and dataclass are both present, prefer dataclass for mutable aggregates (results that may grow) and TypedDict for fixed-shape read-only records. Apply consistently across all public functions in the module.

findings.summary: The Optional[logging.Logger] parameter pattern is applied to some utility functions but not others in logging_utils.py, and the review/generator.py uses module-level logger directly without the option
evidence: logging_utils.py: log_success, log_header, log_progress, log_stage, log_substep, log_operation, log_timing all accept Optional[Logger]
logging_utils.py: log_live_resource_usage (line 385) also accepts Optional[Logger] — consistent
generator.py: log_timeout_info (line 97) uses module-level logger directly with no parameter option, while warmup_model passes logger explicitly via log_success(..., logger)
The inconsistency means callers cannot route generator log output to a different logger
findings.suggestion: Decide on one pattern: either always accept an optional logger parameter for functions that produce user-facing output, or always use the module-level logger. Apply the chosen pattern uniformly across all logging-emitting utility functions.

findings.summary: _REVIEW_TYPE_VALIDATORS dispatch table is rebuilt on every call to validate_review_quality instead of being defined at module level
evidence: Lines 165-171: '_REVIEW_TYPE_VALIDATORS = { ... }' is a local variable inside validate_review_quality, allocated fresh on every invocation
The dict maps string keys to module-level functions that never change — it is a constant that should live at module scope
findings.suggestion: Move _REVIEW_TYPE_VALIDATORS to module level (immediately after the private validator function definitions). This matches the pattern used in coverage_parser.py (lines 124-130: _FAILURE_STRATEGIES module-level list) and makes the dispatch table visible as a design artifact.
